ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Masala Dosa. Nearest parent is a fermented rice pancake or South Indian crepe. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fermented grain products — fermented rice and lentil pancake (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Masala Dosa)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Masala Dosa; A01XS used as closest parent for fermented grain products.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Masala Dosa as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Masala Dosa is approximately 280 g, representing a single dosa crepe with its spiced potato masala filling as typically served at a South Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Masala Dosa — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Food Code EI000039
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for Masala Dosa (Food Code EI000039). Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 where ICMR-NIN did not report these micronutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — dosa (fermented rice and lentil pancake)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 66 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for dosa (fermented rice and lentil pancake) as the closest analogous food. The potato masala filling elevates the effective glycaemic impact. Fermentation moderates but does not substantially lower the GI of the rice-based batter. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (66 × 63.84) / 100 ≈ 42.1 for a 280 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 778 mg per 280 g serving (~34% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Masala Dosa to one serving and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium chutneys, as the cumulative sodium from the dosa, sambar, and chutneys can approach or exceed one-third of the daily sodium limit in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 66 combined with a high glycaemic load of approximately 42 per serving due to the refined rice batter and starchy potato filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Masala Dosa in moderation, limit portion size to half a dosa, pair it with high-fibre sambar and a protein source to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose carefully given the high per-serving glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8 g per 100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and vegan composition with anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, mustard seeds)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Masala Dosa is generally suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease when prepared with minimal oil; the vegan fermented batter provides beneficial lentil protein and the turmeric and mustard seeds in the filling contribute anti-inflammatory phytochemicals, though the moderate sodium content warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vegan and dairy-free; anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric and mustard seeds; fermented batter supports gut microbiome diversity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Masala Dosa is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the turmeric (curcumin) and mustard seeds in the potato filling provide anti-inflammatory benefits, and the fermented batter may support gut microbiome diversity, which is associated with reduced systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala Dosa with Potato Masala Filling is a classic South Indian dish consisting of a large, paper-thin, crisply golden fermented crepe (dosa) made from a batter of soaked and ground rice and urad dal (black gram lentils) that is fermented overnight to develop a characteristic slightly sour flavour and porous, crisp texture. The crepe is cooked on a hot flat griddle (tawa) with oil or ghee until golden-brown and wrapped around a spiced potato masala filling (aloo masala) made from boiled mashed potatoes seasoned with mustard seeds, turmeric, curry leaves, green chilli, and onions. It is traditionally served with coconut chutney and sambar (a lentil and vegetable soup). Nutritionally, Masala Dosa provides approximately 166 kcal per 100 g, with 4.6 g protein, 6.8 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 22.8 g carbohydrate. It is a good source of potassium (265 mg/100 g), vitamin C (7.5 mg/100 g), and B vitamins including thiamin, niacin, and vitamin B6. The dish is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Its glycaemic index is approximately 66 (medium), but the glycaemic load per standard 280 g serving is high (~42), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is one of the most popular breakfast and light meal dishes at South Indian and Tamil restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian diaspora globally.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (Food Code EI000039), which is the primary approved regional source for Indian dishes. GI sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for the closest analogous food (fermented rice and lentil dosa). All core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and internally consistent.
notes: Trans fat is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source for this dish formulation. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as the dish is vegan with no animal-derived fat. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero as the dish contains no animal products (vegan). Vitamin D is confirmed zero as no animal or fortified sources are present. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by ICMR-NIN for this entry; set to -1. Water content not reported by source; set to -1. Copper (0.1 mg/100 g), manganese (0.12 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (1.2 mcg/100 g) supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 where ICMR-NIN did not report these micronutrients. Duplicate portions in the input (three identical '1 serving (200g)' entries) have been deduplicated; the canonical default serving is 280 g per piece with filling. GL per serving = (66 × 22.8 × 2.80) / 100 = 42.1 for a 280 g serving.
